name: Dataset of Raman and SERS spectra of illicit adulterants added to dietary supplements
description: Portable SERS spectra (785 nm, 400–2300 cm⁻¹, 8 cm⁻¹ FWHM, 3 scans) of 11 SERS-active illicit adulterants in dietary supplements. Acquired with a Metrohm Instant SERS analyzer (MISA) using silver printed-SERS (p-SERS) substrates and Orbital Raster Scan (ORS™) technology (30 µm spot, ~2 mm raster, 100 mW, ASTM 1840 calibration). Sample prep: 10 mg/100 µL EtOH, centrifuge 806,400 RCF, 10 µL on strip, dry 15 min, triplicates. Enhancement factors 10³–10⁴. Adulteration LOI 0.1–5.0% w/w in 18 commercial dietary supplements. Developed with the Dutch Food and Consumer Product Safety Authority. Target: compound identity (classification).
citeAs: @article{Rizzo_2023, title={A multi-analyte screening method for the rapid detection of illicit adulterants in dietary supplements using a portable SERS analyzer}, volume={9}, ISSN={2405-8440}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2023.e18509}, DOI={10.1016/j.heliyon.2023.e18509}, number={8}, journal={Heliyon}, publisher={Elsevier BV}, author={Rizzo, Serena and Weesepoel, Yannick and Erasmus, Sara and Sinkeldam, Joost and Piccinelli, Anna Lisa and van Ruth, Saskia}, year={2023}, month=aug, pages={e18509}}
citation: Rizzo, S., Weesepoel, Y., Erasmus, S., Sinkeldam, J., Piccinelli, A. L., van Ruth, S. (2023). Dataset of Raman and Surface-enhanced Raman Spectroscopy spectra of illicit adulterants added to dietary supplements. Wageningen University & Research. https://doi.org/10.17632/y4md8znppn/1

distribution.name: Dataset of Raman and SERS spectra of illicit adulterants added to dietary supplements
distribution.description: Source data hosted at the original repository.

field.name: spectrum_id
field.description: Sequential integer index identifying each spectrum.

field.name: raman_shift_cm_inv
field.description: Raman shift wavenumber axis in cm⁻¹. Shape: (n_wavenumbers,).

field.name: spectrum_intensity
field.description: Raman scattering intensity in arbitrary units. Shape: (n_wavenumbers,) per spectrum.

field.name: target
field.description: Class label: string identifier of the sample class.

rai:dataCollection: Portable SERS spectroscopy. Metrohm Instant SERS analyzer (MISA), 785 nm ± 0.5 nm excitation, Orbital Raster Scan (ORS™) technology, 30 µm spot continuously rasterised over ~2 mm diameter, 400–2300 cm-1, 8 cm-1 FWHM resolution, 100 mW laser power, 3 scans averaged per spectrum, silver printed-SERS (p-SERS) substrates, ASTM 1840 calibration standard. Sample prep — 10 mg dissolved in 100 µL ethanol, vortex 20 s, centrifuge 806,400 RCF for 2 min, 10 µL supernatant pipetted onto silver p-SERS strip, dried 15 min, measured in triplicate.
rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: SERS spectra acquired via MISA Cal software in Smart Acquire mode (laser power level 5, auto-integration, averages of 3 scans, raster on). HQI matching performed against the built spectral library. Secondary Pearson correlation analysis performed in R (prospectr, signal, Rtools packages) on first- derivative spectra. No explicit baseline correction described for the library spectra; raw spectra with three replicates per adulteration level.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Compound identity labels assigned from known analytical-grade reference standards. 11 SERS-active adulterants (out of 23 tested) selected for library building based on confirmed SERS activity on silver printed-SERS substrates — acetildenafil, fluoxetine HCl, homo sildenafil, melatonin, phenethylamine, piperin, sildenafil citrate, synephrine, thiosildenafil, vardenafil HCl, vinpocetine. Enhancement factors 10³–10⁴ confirmed. Library built in MISA Cal software; HQI similarity scores used for identification (red >0.95, orange >0.85, yellow >0.75).
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information is included. All spectra are from pure analytical-grade chemical standards and commercial dietary supplement products without any human identifiers.
rai:dataBiases: Library spectra represent pure analytical-grade standards in ethanol; real dietary supplement excipients and plant-based extracts may interfere. The 11-compound panel is limited to SERS-active RASFF-flagged targets; novel analogues or SERS-inactive compounds cannot be identified from this library alone. Silver p-SERS substrates are single-use and non-reusable; SERS substrate batch variability can affect reproducibility. Gold p-SERS substrates also tested for SERS-inactive compounds.
rai:dataLimitations: Small SERS library (11 reference compounds); insufficient for data-driven ML without augmentation. LOI for adulteration detection is 0.1–5.0% w/w in commercial dietary supplements (18 commercial products tested). SERS-inactive compounds (AND, DMAA, DMAE, DMBA, TSIL, VAR, XAN) cannot be identified with this method and require the complementary FT-Raman dataset. Substrate adsorption selectivity and solubility in ethanol affect SERS activity per compound.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Enables customs inspectors and food safety officers to perform on-site screening of dietary supplements for undeclared pharmaceutical adulterants within 20 minutes, with no skilled operators required. Developed in collaboration with the Dutch Food and Consumer Product Safety Authority. Supports EU RASFF rapid alert enforcement and consumer protection from unsafe supplement adulteration.
rai:dataUseCases: Portable SERS spectral library matching, dietary supplement food-fraud screening, adulterant identification, benchmarking of portable vs. benchtop Raman performance, detection-limit studies for pharmaceutical compound mixtures.
